title: Choosing the Right Solar Inverter for Your Needs
author: Admin
excerpt: Learn about the critical aspects of selecting a solar inverter for your residential, commercial, or utility-scale project.

blocks.text: Choosing the right solar inverter is a crucial decision for your solar energy system. Inverters are the 'brain' of the solar project, responsible for converting the direct current (DC) electricity generated by solar panels into alternating current (AC) electricity used by home appliances and the grid.
When selecting a solar inverter, key considerations include the capacity of your solar panel array, grid compatibility, efficiency ratings, temperature coefficient, and advanced monitoring systems.

left.text: Best suited for large rooftops with minimal shading. They offer high efficiency and lower cost per watt, making them the go-to choice for utility and commercial installations.

right.text: Ideal for complex rooftops with shading or multiple orientations. Each panel operates independently, maximizing overall system yield even in partial shade conditions.

items: Single-phase string inverters — 1kW to 10kW for homes
Three-phase string inverters — 10kW to 250kW for commercial projects
Hybrid inverters with built-in MPPT battery charging
Utility-grade central inverters for MW-scale solar farms

blocks.caption: SunLynk distributes a complete range of bankable inverter solutions

title: Why Class-A Weather Monitoring is Essential for Utility PV
author: SunLynk Editorial
excerpt: Precise meteorological data is key to calculating performance ratio (PR). Discover why Class-A pyranometers and weather monitoring stations are required by grid operators.

blocks.text: In a utility-scale solar plant, every percentage point of Performance Ratio (PR) directly translates to revenue. A PR of 79% vs 81% on a 100MW plant can mean hundreds of thousands of dollars in annual lost production. The accuracy of your irradiance measurements is the single largest non-engineering variable in this equation.

The ISO 9060:2018 standard classifies pyranometers into Class A, B, and C based on their measurement uncertainty, directional response, and temperature dependence. Class-A instruments offer the highest accuracy with uncertainties below 2% — mandatory for grid operator reporting and lender technical due diligence.

items.caption: Class-A Weather Station at a 50MW Solar Plant
Bifacial Sensors for Rear Irradiance Measurement

items: At minimum one Class-A pyranometer in the plane-of-array (POA)
Data logger with <1% measurement error and cellular/RS485 connectivity

left.text: Financial lenders require at least 12 months of Class-A irradiance data for accurate P90 yield assessment. SunLynk's weather stations have been accepted by all major lenders for project bankability.

right.text: Real-time soiling loss detection, shading analysis, and fault identification — SunLynk's SCADA dashboard integrates directly with Class-A sensors for live PR calculation.

title: Integrating Battery Storage (BESS) with SCADA for Grid Stability
author: Tech Team
excerpt: Hybrid solar systems combining battery energy storage require intelligent SCADA dispatch algorithms. We analyze how Modbus control loops maintain zero-export grid compliance.

blocks.text: As solar penetration in grid-tied systems increases, the challenge of managing surplus generation and peak demand becomes critical. Battery Energy Storage Systems (BESS) integrated via SCADA are emerging as the definitive solution to this challenge.

Many industrial and commercial consumers face zero-export requirements from their DISCOM — they can generate solar energy but cannot feed back to the grid. Without a BESS, this means curtailing solar generation during off-peak load periods. With BESS + SCADA, excess generation charges batteries instead of going to waste.

blocks.alt: BESS integrated with a commercial solar installation
blocks.caption: A 500kWh BESS system integrated with SunLynk's SCADA at a manufacturing plant

items: Grid meter measures import/export in real time (±1% accuracy CT sensors)
SCADA reads grid power via Modbus RTU/TCP at 1-second intervals
Dispatch algorithm computes optimal charge/discharge setpoint
BESS BMS receives setpoint via Modbus → executes in <500ms
Solar inverter clipping is avoided by directing surplus to BESS first

left.text: SCADA identifies peak demand periods from historical load profiles. Battery discharges before the peak window to reduce maximum demand charges — one of the largest cost centers for commercial consumers.

right.text: During grid outages, the SCADA initiates island mode within 20ms. The BESS sustains critical loads while solar continues generating — providing seamless, uninterrupted power to your facility.

comments.author: GridEngineer
comments.text: The Modbus dispatch latency figures here are very helpful. We're designing a 1MW/2MWh system and were unsure about the control loop timing requirements. This clears it up.
